speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How did he lose it?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: He had to sell it. He didn't, uh, he couldn't keep it all.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did he sell it to the bank?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I don't remember when he sold it to him. I was pretty young then.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: So how much acreage was he able to keep?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: He must have had about 160.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Now I'm not sure, but I think so around there some way. So, and he had quite a bit more than that, but when he lost this, he just had this smaller acreage.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was there any feeling of the community around Harvard before the town?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Oh yes.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: They, I can remember my folks, bonding us kids all up in the wagon or a sled or someplace and going away and staying Thursday days with some of the neighbors. And they'd come to our place and stay Thursday days.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: And you'd play with the kids?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Mm -hmm.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: And we had little schools and community meds there. We'd have box socials and dances.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Box socials? What are they?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Don't you know how they are?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What you bring food?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: You'd make a real fancy box and decorate it all up with pretty flowers and everything and then the men would bid on them and find them. And whos ever name was in it, that's the one they ate their supper with. The lunch that was in the box.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: You never heard of those?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: No, I've heard of them, but I've never heard of them being around here.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Oh yes, used to have them real often.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: This thing goes, yeah, it's making a lot of noise. I don't know what's around it, it had to blow up or something.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Now what would happen, so they, was that just for single people or was it?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Oh, everybody would.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: So you could get stuck with a 16, I mean a 60 year old girl or something. I didn't make it there for somebody care in those days, it was just there for fun. And they had their Christmas programs and things like that. That was a long time before the railroad came through there.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: There was a school I heard from before the railroad came through. The Little Rock Schoolhouse. So that was right on your own step. Right on this.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: It was about all that we had to go to school, maybe half a month.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were there many people in the area then?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Oh, quite a few. I can't remember how many we had in school. There was four of us and two, three of the Smiths, two or three Howls, and Horn Bay's, Daley's, oh, I forgot a few around there.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: They came to school.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: And at one time, the chambers flat district and ours were so hard up that they had school three months at one place and three months at the other. Their youngsters would come to our school for three months and then we would go up their school for three months. That's the only way they could pay for their teachers.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: My horseback.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I don't know if you know where the chambers flat is or not.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Isn't that towards Princeton?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: That's down in the right in the Butterfielders. That's right. There was a little old schoolhouse there.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: We went to.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do you remember much about things that happened in school? Well, what happened in school? Games or anything that you pull or have hold of in your home?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Just the usual things that happened in school. We were kind of just ordinary kids just like they are nowadays. I remember we used to get the teacher to excuse us. We'd tell the folks when we used to come home and we'd take our horses and go up in the woods and play hooking all night long after.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: We didn't tell our folks about it until a long time after we were married. We'd tell Dad about it one thing and he said, I can't understand the kids.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were you pretty close to your sisters?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: In age?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: No, I mean as a family.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Oh yes. Families were pretty close in those days.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you have any chores to do around the house?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: We didn't have any brothers.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: So we had to help with the milking and digging the chickens and things like that. We all had our work run. In those days I think everybody did. My Dad couldn't do it alone so we had to help him.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were there any specific things that you'd do?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: What do you mean?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: As chores, you know, jobs that you particularly had to do.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Well, milk cows. Bring in the milk and put it in the pans. We didn't have separators in those days. They had great big pans and we would put the milking out and let it settle and cream came to the top and skim off the cream.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How long would it set?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Usually all night. Or it could set it there to make a difference. Mother and Dad had built a cellar and covered the inside of it with cheesecloth to keep the dirt. See it was an underground cellar to keep the dirt from falling through. And she had a big table in the cellar and that's where the milk set.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: She'd skim it off every day and put it in a large jar to churn. And then when Dad would go to Blues, she'd send the butter in. She'd made it and she got 10 cents a pound for it.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: She sent eggs, butter, and booze.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would the butter keep longer?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I think he probably went in but every week.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: They'd keep it in the cellar after it had been churned?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: The cellar was just real cold. It was done in the dirt.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was it under the house? Was it under the house?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: No, it was out. Just at the back door and it sat in a bank. I remember we kids always liked to slide down the, you know, we'd get up there and slide out and the Mother and Dad caught us. We were in bed because it knocked the dirt all down inside.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do you remember how skating in Harvard was that? Did you skate on the creek much?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: We skated all winter. We had good skating all the time. That's where we spent all of our time in the wintertime.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Nights.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: We went out every night. All weekends. We were never home weekends. Go down on the river and building fire and stay there.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: After they put the dam in, then they flooded the river above the dam. Boy, we had real good skating then.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: The dam where?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Potlights put the dam in there at Harvard to hold the water to drive the logs down.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I didn't even know there was a dam.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: You didn't know there was a dam there?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: No. Well, it was just a small dam, you know, the potlights you put in.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: And it was to help them float the logs down?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: So that was really early on they put that in.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: They always, every spring they had logs right. I remember that real well, how they used to bring the logs down. And then when the potlights built this dam, then they could hold the water back, you know, and pour more in potlights. Easy then.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How did the logs get over the dam?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: They went through. There was gates that they could lift up.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Now where was this?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: It isn't there anymore. It was on Dad's old place. It was right in Harvard then. Yeah, just above Harvard there. It was almost exactly where our whole house sat. Our whole house sat up from the river. There's, I don't know if you ever noticed the railroad there at Harvard where the first cut is there at Harvard. Railroad cut. Well, I don't know how it sat right there where that railroad cut is. And we was just up from the river to Spoolways. And the dam was right in that place. I think it's by the dam left there now.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I'm not sure.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I don't know that country well enough. No.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: You'd have to go up there and just walk around. That's a Montelier.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: So would you have to clear the ice to be able to escape a lot of this?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Well, they didn't use it the other time you see the dam. I think they left it open in the wintertime. A lot of the time. I know one time we had good skiing there and they let the water out of the dam. We just had ice every way.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: It really buckled.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you ever fall in? Did you fall in much?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: No, but a couple boys did. I never did fall in. One day a friend of mine and I were skating and we were acting smart. These two boys came along behind us acting like we were, of course, silly. And all of us, we got over it because we didn't weigh as much as they did. And when they came along they went in. Boy, we had enough time getting one of them out.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Why'd you have trouble? He kept breaking through?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: He just his head was sticking out. But we finally got him. The other boy that was skating with him didn't go in. And the three of us got him out. We didn't skate anymore that day. We went home.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: He's still living. He lives in Vancouver. And he came down not very long ago and I asked him if he remembered any color ever. He said he sure did.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you toboggan much or anything like that?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Right. In Harvard.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: There was a hill that goes up where we used to live. And that got real slippery in the wintertime when we used to slide there all day and all night.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would a lot of kids get together?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Oh, yeah. All the kids around there would come in.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: We had one man that lived, he came there from Minnesota. And he was a married man. He had four or five children. He used to come in and skate. He loved to skate because he was such a wonderful skater when he used to. He'd come in and skate with us kids and just have a big time. He was a beautiful skater.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: It sounds like there was somewhat of a community there even before Harvard got there. Obvious.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I think there always is in a little settlement, you know, where there's families. I think they more or less make their own fun. They did in those days. It'd be hard to do now because the kids wouldn't be happy with that. It'd be pretty slow for them.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you as a child ever hear rumors about the mill opening up or anything like that? I mean, do people get pretty excited??

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: About what?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: About the mill and the railroad and everything that was going to happen?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Oh, I guess we were all happy when it came in there.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I know my dad was. Very happy about it. I think everyone was.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: It tells you in that little pamphlet about Princeton being in there before the college came in.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Not bad, I know.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Princeton was about our only, was the only town we had around there. Closest was an ex and it was 20 miles away. And there was nothing in Princeton but a store and a couple of sluoms and all. I think a dance hall.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: A few families. They just had one store in Princeton.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: And they had a hotel. I don't know if that hotel, I think it was put in after the lumberjack. Oh, I don't know. It might have been there before the lumberjack started coming in. I believe it was there, yeah, it was. It was Princeton, they called it the halfway house between Clos and the Houdos. See, the reminding of the Houdos. They'd mind up there for year and year and year. And this little town of Princeton was the happy place. See, it was all horses then. And they had to have a place to stay. They couldn't make it in one day.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do you know anything about the mines and the dreams and the stories about the mines?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Well, I know they had mines up there and I remember the Chinese used to come in there and stop at our place to inquire the way the mines. They brought in lots of Chinese to mine.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do you remember any stories about anything happened?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Because I remember there was a story about them.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: They killed a bunch of white men who went in and killed five or six Chinese one night.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: That and a lot was over.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: That's when I was just a little, and I just couldn't remember hearing them talk about it. I don't know why they killed them with the dead.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do you know why the Chinese disappeared from the area?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I don't suppose they got enough to get them to stay. I don't think it was very rich mining in there.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: There were several old miners that spent their entire life up there, and they made enough to keep them, I guess. It's the only way they had them making a living.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How were they?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Well, I can't remember their names. Edna probably could tell you. Because her folks used to live right up in there close to where they mined, and she probably could kill all their names.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do you remember hearing anything of the Duff Wagner feud? Did you know about that?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: What happened?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: I'm not sure. That's why I'm asking you. It's something Glenn told me that I ought to ask Edna Butterfield, and she didn't remember.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Wagner?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Duff Wagner. They were two. Oh, yes. I knew both of them. Charlie Wagner and Billy Duff. Yeah, I knew both of those guys.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Did they have a feud?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Supposedly.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: They lived pretty close together. Yeah, I think that's hard. A lot that they would have, all right. They were pretty. Mr. Duff was quite a little guy. But I didn't know that they... There was Johnny English. He lived up in there.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Did you hear about him?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: He was a little Frenchman. He lived up in there for years and years and years, and then let's see. There's Billy Duff and Charlie Wagner. Johnny English. I can't think of that other man.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were they all Bachelors?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Uh -huh. All Bachelors.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were they Sarah Keirson or something?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: They were Peter Offner.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Can't think of that love man's name.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: But there was...

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Oh, what was that old fella's name then?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: He used a Chewy's Tabasco. And then he had an old fireplace in his cabin. And he had a little fireplace in his cabin. And then he would take the cud and lay it up on his mantle over his fireplace and let it dry, and then he'd smoke it.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: He was quite a saving. I can't think of his name now. We used to go up there and visit him a lot. We'd go up there camping right close to where he was working.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Where would you camp? Where would you go?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: All up in the woods up there. We'd go up at Huckleberry. There were days of gone. Orgo fishing up in there on the river.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would you get a lot of fish?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Yes. There was good fishing in there then. The roads weren't good like they are now. You had to go in with a buggy.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Horses.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: We'd take our camping outfits and go in there and stay three or four days, maybe a week.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: The whole family.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Dad would take us in sometimes and leave us in there. Three or four days.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Just the kids? Just the kids? Were you ever afraid of being left alone?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Yes. I was scared.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I remember one time we went up there and... There was a lady from Spokane that came down. We all went up in there and Dad took us up. He helped us put up our tent and get all fixed up. He came home. We stayed and went up to Waring.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: They were all afraid to sleep at the door, so they made me sleep there. The door of the tent. They put the axe beside me.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: You hear a noise?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: They hit him with the axe. We used to bed and sleep and the little girl that was with us come up. She stepped on my sister's foot. She let me yell out her and she just knew that it was a cougar. We'd heard a cougar the night before. Up above our camp. It wasn't anything, so we... everything was alright.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: That'd be pretty scary.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: It's pretty lonesome when you're up there, a bunch of women all by yourself.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Especially in those days when you couldn't... you know, when they were so...

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: No bones, no nothing up there. That was a lot of fun.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would you pick a lot of hucleberries?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Oh yes, goodness. We used to bring them out to the cub school. We'd put the... take a great big wash tub and put it in a spring or a little creek, whatever it was, handy. And then for our berries in there, they'd keep for a week some cold.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Bring them out and get them. Jam and jelly.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Do you like huckelberries?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Yeah, I think they make the best jam and jelly berries. I bet they do. They're fine.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: We used to put up so many of them.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you make huckleberry juice much? You know, just for uses.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: They used to make huckleberry wine. It was good. Well, it was sure strong though.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: They used to be a... no...

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Bastion lived up in Plum Harbor.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: They called him Hobble Gubbles. I remember Rodney's first name was there, what his name was. One day, a bunch of us went up there. We kids... he brought out his juleine. Of course, we girls didn't drink very much, but the boys, they drank quite a bit. We had quite a time going home.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: What's going to stay on their arses? Why is it sure strong?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did most people make one?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: We never made any. Maybe lots of people didn't, but I don't think so. This old man did. He threw out the cup of his makeup. He lived there by himself.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: It sounds like you had a pretty nice childhood. It sounds like you had a really good childhood.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: We did. Best of any. Usually on Sundays, we'd take our horses and go out into the camera and be gone all day.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Run races and jump logs. We really had fun.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You each had your own horse?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Everybody had a horse.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I remember one time, mother and dad had gone Spokane.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: And...

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: there were three of us, my sister and I, and another girl. Took our horses and we were out carrying around through the woods. We were running a race. And this girl, she had a great big horse. And he hit my sister's horse with his shoulder and knocked her down and threw her sister off. And she had a concussion. And we didn't know what to do for her. We got her home. We got her on the horse and got her home. She didn't come to for about three or four days.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: She just laid there?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Just laid there.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Kids were all alone. Mother and dad were gone. We didn't know what to do. So we just slept in bed and took care of her.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How'd you take care of her?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: We slept in there. We didn't have any medicine or anything. She came out of it. She didn't know what she was talking about. She was out of her head.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: When she was lying or when she came to.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You mean she'd talk?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: It's a wonder to me she didn't die.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: She didn't.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Nowadays they'd have you in the hospital for a month.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: We didn't know what to do. They're just the. Four of us at home. And mother and dad we couldn't get touch of them there in Spokane. But it sure scared me. She came out of it just fine. When the folks came to go home I see it's okay.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: That seems unusual for them to you know.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did they go on trips like that often?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: We had lots of neighbors so.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: It wasn't.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: And we could do the charges same as they could. They had to get away once in a while.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Kids knew how to take care of themselves those days.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: There wasn't anybody to.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Maybe you had to look after yourself.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you all learn how to cook and everything like that?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I think.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Did you learn how to did you would your mother teach you how to sew or. Would you learn.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: She but she does. Now I always did hate so. I never did like to sew my sisters all four of them are good. They can just really make anything and I can't sew at all. Because I didn't like it. Just hated so and I still do.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Do you remember any of the things that. That your mother used to make.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: That are really different from things that you do. Mother was a wonderful cook. You mean in cooking on. Oh I think. She could do anything. She made her own soap and she made. Everything. Did all of our sewing.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: She didn't have too much to.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: You know. To work with.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Of course she had all of her own butter and cream and milk.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Cookies.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Shortcake and. She's making most delicious biscuits. They can follow biscuits.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: She had bacon powder.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: How'd you remember all that in your head if she didn't use a. Just natural in those days I guess. I had to remember.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: When.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: The potlatch was spilling the railroad through Harvard.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: That was the end of the line then they had. But it cares to remove yet. And mother of lorded. These loggers. Things that came in there. People that came up the end of the line came up there to eat. And we did have. Oh goodness I don't know how many we would have. Mother fed them. And she cooked all the time. She was really busy.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Having to yell.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Do you remember much about the. The laying of the track and the building at the railroad.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Yeah that's quite interesting.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Today on the camp near Harvard.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: It had camps all along the railroad. There was a big camp right down. Across the field from where we lived.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: And I used to go with dad to deliver milk. He furnished the camp in milk. I used to deliver it. So I remember that quite well.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: What kind of place did they have set up for the. Tents.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Great big tents.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were there a lot of men working on them?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Mm hmm.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Yeah I was like yeah. I don't know how many. To me it seemed big.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did they work right through the winter?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: They surely didn't. I don't think they could.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I don't remember but I don't think they could. Snow got too deep. They might have I don't know.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Well I doubt it.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Of course they could work from early spring to late in the fall. Probably a few months they didn't work.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Can you tell me the story of how Harvard got its name and you know how they picked the site for it?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: It's in that building. I know but I just wanted to tell you as you remember it.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Well I know they put the town in there or when they put the depot in there. They were talking to Dad about it and they wanted to call it Canfield. And Dad said no. That he didn't want it named Canfield. He said the Harvard's right down here or the Prince's down here about six miles. He said let's call it Harvard. So they did. And then when the Camp Blair was put in up there they wanted to call that Canfield Park and he wouldn't want to do that either. He said no Mr. Laird had helped so much on the parks and called Laird for it. That's what it's called.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Now did he donate the land for the town site?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Dad did.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I don't know how much but it's all on his land.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: And he just gave it to them.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: There was a hotel, a living stable, and a store.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I guess that was all, I guess, a blacksmith's shop.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: And a few houses. The depot. There was a boarding house there.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: In addition to the hotel?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: There was a French lady that came in there and she had a boarding house. She boarded a lot of men.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: And then they built a new school and brought it down closer to Harvard.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Who would stay in the boarding house? Would it be the loggers?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Yes, there was lots of logging around there then. That was all new timber in there. They put a railroad track through my dad's field up into the way back in timber and brought out timber.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: They had a hard little boy to keep the cattle out of the field. And my sister and I went up there and we played up there all day with this little boy. He didn't want to stay there long, so he stepped there all day.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What was he supposed to keep the cattle from going in the field?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: You see, the railroad went right through the field. And the cattle then those days were turned loose out on the range. And there wasn't someone there to watch them on. They were all in the field. So he kept them out.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: For most of those loggers, single men that just...

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I imagine they were. Well, I suppose there were a lot of married men. I don't know.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: You didn't get to know very much. Oh, I knew a lot of them, yeah.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: There was quite a few young men came in there from... Oh, Minnesota and Wisconsin and all those eastern places, you know, where they did so much logging. My brother -in -law was from...

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I almost made that place. Someplace in Wisconsin. He's just a young man. He came out here and married my sister. And then my older sister's husband came from...

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Minnesota, I believe.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: So there's lots of people came from the east to work out here in the woods.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: But they...

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: They just...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: They'd come out here and then would they try to make the area their home once they got here?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: A lot of them, their parents came.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I know my sister's husband heard his parents came. And then my family moved out.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did all those businesses that you mentioned in Harvard, did those get going right away? Did they just start right up?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I remember building them. They got a lot pretty fast. Of course, they weren't much to look at after. But they held people and fed the men. The hotel was a pretty good size. I think there was three floors. I remember.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Who stayed there?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: The men.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: And they had camps out around Harvard. And if they went out on the train or anything when they came back, they were not before they got out of camp. Because the camps were quite a ways away, a lot of them.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: So there weren't that many people?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I mean, the loggers wouldn't just stay there and work out of the hotel.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What did the store have that was in Harvard?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Oh, it had everything in it.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Whitaker and Steele was their names, and they will forget them when they first came in there. Whitaker's had a family, and Mr. Steele was a single man. They built a hole in there. Whitaker's did. Names there are several years.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did they sell what, groceries?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Oh, they had groceries, and they had a lot of hardware, you know, that everyone would want. Around there. It wasn't a great big store, but it had a lot of stuff.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were there any saloons in town?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: We didn't have a saloon in Harvard.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Why not?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I don't know. Whether dad kept them out a lot. Maybe he wouldn't let them have a lot. I don't know. But anyway, they started one after Harvard was, a long time after Harvard started, and they were going to have a saloon in there. I don't know what ever happened to it. Never did materialize, I don't think.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I never knew it.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did your father have control over what went in there?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I don't know. I don't know why it was, I don't suppose he did.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was he opposed to drinking?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Yeah, he never did like drinking.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Princeton had two saloons.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Three, I think.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did your father ever give a reason? I mean, what was his reason for why drink was so bad?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I don't know. He never liked it. He never drank himself. He didn't like to be around anyone that was drinking. Of course, I suppose always men, when they came through Princeton, loaded up, I probably didn't keep it out of Harvard. I know when the train stopped there at Harvard, that was the last town for the railroad they had gone on through. And these men had come up from blues and potlots and Princeton. They'd all be drunk. And the conductor fell off and lay him on the platform at the depot, let him sleep it off, and he could get up and take care of themselves. I've been out there lots of times when the platform was just full of drug men lying there, all dead drunk.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: They wouldn't pick him up in town and take him out there, would they?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: They'd stay there and they'd slap him off.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would they pick him up from the towns?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: No, they'd get on the train. They'd get on the train and be drunk the time they got up there. They probably had to help him on, too, in some of those places. He had them off there.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do you remember any of the celebrations that people had, you know, 4th of July?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: We used to go to Princeton all the time, to their celebrations, until Harvard got started and then we had them up there.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What would they, what would you have?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: All they'd have speakers come in from different places, and they'd have dances and races, and they usually had a parade with a few floats in it, and just an old -time celebration.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What would they make the floats out of?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Oh, it was usually on an old wagon, made of a bunting they called then, and kept riding on it.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I know I used to play for the dances. I played in an orchestra.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What did you play? What did you play?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I played piano. We'd play. We usually had about two or three days of it. We'd start in the afternoon, play till time to eat, and then to start again and play till way up in the morning, and then go home and sleep a little while and go back and start in again.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: This would be for 4th of July?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Hard work.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What kind of music would you play?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Oh, we had waltzes and foxtrots and square dances, everything.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: It was quite a thing. I remember one time, Dad built a great big open -air pavilion, and we played out there. That was nice. It was cool out there.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How many other people would there be in the orchestra?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Four or five. We had violins, and Dad played trumpet. We had a railroad man that played clarinet.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Quite a bunch of us.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you learn how to play the piano yourself? Did you? You taught yourself?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Well, yeah. You might say I did. I had a few lessons when I was about 9 years old. I think I had six lessons. I learned the notes. But my Dad was quite musical, so he could help me.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you play a lot of the dances?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I played all my life. Ever since I was about 12 years old, I played for dances.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would you get any kind of pay for the play?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I almost get paid.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did they pass around a hat, or would they pay you a hip -top?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Well, usually for big dances like that, they had a price. But I played for lots of dances where they passed the hat.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How to keep yourself from dancing?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I couldn't ever get away.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: When the railroad went through to Boville, we went up there every Friday night, I guess it was. We played up there every Friday night for years. We'd get on the train at Harvard at 6. It would take about an hour to go to Boville. We'd start playing about 8 and play until about 5 in the morning. Then we would go down and the train would be ready to pull out and pull out at 7. Then we'd go on the morning train.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: This was you and your father, right? Where would you play?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: They had a big hall up there. There was a fellow who played the violin from Harvard. My dad and I, and there was maybe some musicians from pollution playing with us.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Who would be the women who would be in the dances? I mean, there weren't that many women around Boville, were there?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Oh, there was then when the railroad went in.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: There were lots of homes in there then. People moved in there pretty fast after the town started. They had a quite nice little town there. They had a bank and all. They had lots of stores. It was a real nice little town.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were there ever any fights or any trouble in the dances?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I don't think I heard a paper dance in my life. It wasn't a fight.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What were they fighting about? Do you remember any of the fights?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Just drinking.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Get the drinking. You know, your feelings are always hurt when you're drinking. Someone will touch you while you hit it. I don't think they knew what they were fighting about.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did they ever get pretty big?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Not like you see in the movies. And usually just two.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: But there's always fights.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was there still a lot of drink around even if prohibition went in?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: That didn't stop it. When I lived down in Southern Idaho, down around Cascade and down in there, there was steel in every house. It wasn't as bad then as it had been. They said every place along that railroad there was steel. I believe it. That was quite a good place down there.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I don't know if there was ever much moonshine right around in here or not, around Harvard and Princeton. I just don't know. I think there was some. There could have been.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were there ever you saw Indians very much when you were young?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Indians in that area.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: We've gotten lots of mornings when we were little kids. We had a little field right up from the house, and that would just be full of Indians digging camas. They took that root, made flour out of it. I think they said they used to. Dad never said a word. He just let them dig. The fields were just blue with that camas in those days. I don't see much of it anymore. But they'd come in there and never ask for anything. Just go ahead and get it.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did they just set up camp right out from here?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: No. They'd just dig it and go to their camps, wherever they were. They didn't stay up in here too much in the wintertime, so they didn't have any permanent camps up in here. They would come up in the summer to fish and hunt and pick huckleberries. But their winter camps were down around Lewiston and down in that country where it was warmer. Not so much snow.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You never had much dealings with them yourself?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: No, I was scared to death of them. Why, I don't know.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: But I just didn't want to be around them. Daddy, he always liked to talk to them. They liked Dad, very friendly, but I was just scared of them. They smelled funny. They smelled smoky. I was afraid of them. But they were very friendly.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I guess my grandmother used to have a lot of time with them, don't fall actually, when they first come in there. They'd come to their place and they'd want everything grandmother had. They'd want to take this, they'd want to take that. She had a lot of time with them. But we never did have a problem.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do you remember, did you go into the sail days at the work? Hot lads?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Always. Never missed one.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What were they like?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I just heard... They were quite a celebration.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: They had these big sails, and they'd have lots of drawings and different things. I remember one time they gave a good price for the biggest family. Not in numbers, but in weight. And there was one family there. All of them were big family. The boys were big and the girls were big. They took the prize and they won't forget that. We used to have some big times there.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did they have pretty good sails that everyone went in?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Yes, they did.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: They had the biggest crowds. Everybody from the country. The trains ran in there then in the potlads. Everybody from up around Boville and Derry and everybody would go down there. They'd run a special train at night, take them home after the dance was over.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: They did the dance?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Oh, they always had a great big dance.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: We'd always go down on a train, usually. We'd go down in the morning and then come back on that train. A bunch of us kids.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: I wanted to ask, was your mother ever afraid when your father was away in those ugly years?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I imagine she was. I know she always had the shotgun where she could get it pretty handy.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: She had quite a life there. I bet she was frightened to death half the time.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I know one time she had the office time with a neighbor's old bull. She was milking cows, mother was. We had a lane that went out quite a ways. To get out to pasture. It was an open lane so that this old bull could come in there. He'd come in and he heard our cows and let them go up pasture. He'd make them stay there all day. Mother was just about half afraid of them. She was afraid to go out there and take after him. She got her shotgun. She went out there and she left me having it. He took off and never did come back. This neighbor that he belonged to, he knew who did it. He knew that mother was having trouble with him. He was getting mothered by it. It took him a long time to figure out all the shot out of him. They didn't come back anymore. She must have had quite a life. I imagine she did.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: She couldn't have been too badly frightened because we kids never felt it. I think if she'd been terribly frightened we'd have felt it too.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: That was the way your father earned most of his money in the early years. Later on then he started to log. He didn't have to do that.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: He logged his place or he worked long time.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I remember him logging with Oxum.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: That's what he started with then?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: He used them very long. They're so slow. I remember one time going up in the woods and watching him log with Oxum. That's the only time I remember just that one time. After that, well then he got horses. He did a lot of logging then. He worked in some of the lumber cans too. One time he went out and worked in a shingle mill. He lost his finger. He got his finger off.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: This one.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: He was just learning to play the violin. Trying to learn to play the violin. So that stopped. Just playing the violin.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Well in most days you didn't have to have too much money. You raised everything.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: You had your meat and all your cream and butter and milk and things like that. But all you had to buy was salt and sugar and flour. I remember when they used to take in wheat and trade for flour.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would they get back white flour?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Uh -huh.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: They done that.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Not too awful long ago. I remember people doing that during their week for flour.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did your father grow much wheat?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Well he had his own cattle and his pigs and chickens. Every time meat there was. Oh, wheat. Not too much wheat. He did a little. Enough for his own feed. Mostly oats I think. When he first started. But then after he got more land cleared, then he raised more wheat. Raised some peas.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you have an ice house? Did you have an ice house?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Always.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Dad put up lots of ice.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Where would you cut it? Where would you get it?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: On the river.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: He'd get some man and they'd take the sled and go down and cut it. Great big blocks and be felt that thick. And put it in the sled and bring it up. They buried it in sawdust. And it kept all summer.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: And then they had the, well just like your refrigerator is now. It would be cold in there all the time. It was lined with enamel and had shelves in it. And that ice in the top came, melded as it came down and it melded and went back. And dripped in a pan underneath. And if you didn't forget it, that thing was running over all the time.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Where did you keep that? Right in the house?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: In the pantry.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: So we thought we were just real lucky when we got that.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Oh you didn't have that in your ears.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: We didn't have electricity in the air hole. Oh my goodness.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: That needed electricity and icebox?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: It was just ice.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I don't know when electricity came in here. I don't remember when the REA came in. You probably, probably someone has told you. But it hasn't been too long. I don't know when it came into Harvard. When Harvard got electricity. I don't know either. I know Dad got, seems to me like they had it before Dad died. And he died in 42, I believe.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Oh yeah, I never had it before Dad.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Dad like Christian before he died because he said that one time that he, when he went in there, there was nothing. There wasn't even any roads that you could hardly travel on. Mostly trails. And he said everything came to it. The railroad, everything. Delbone, electricity. And he was fortunate that way. I don't know why he ever took up a homestead up in that place. When there was lots of land out around Poulouse and around to there he could have taken. But he liked it up in Timbre. It was pretty up in there then. Just solid timber.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Beautiful.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: My mother and dad were married. He went up on a horse and married her and she had a horse. And he built a little house and took her in there. And they went in on horseback and my sisters got a little clock, something like this one on it smaller. My mother carried that. She got it for wedding gift. She carried it on a horseback in her new home.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: That was all she did?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Well I suppose they took the rest of it later but she wanted that clock so she took it with her.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: The roads weren't very good in there then. I don't know if that or it was just a trail. I don't know.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: That was a long time ago.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: When was it that you're talking about? When did your parents get married with?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I'll see. 80.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I have it written down. 85 or 6. See if me like it was.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: So that's about all he came. That's right. Your father came from California.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: He was born in Massachusetts.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did he go out for the gold rush?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: He was just a kid and he wanted to get away from home. Until he left 19 he was when he came out.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: And how did he end up here?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Well he just started working around and he wanted to work on the railroad so he was working on the railroad. He worked out of Spokane and when they spring that railroad from Spokane to Sprague he worked on that. And he said he could have bought all that land around Spokane for $5. Wasn't any town there then. Just a little one. Here he come way up there at Harvard.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Almost did it.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: My dad liked me where there was lots of excitement and where there was things to do you know. We loved that.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was he involved in the politics of the area?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Yeah. He was a very very staunch Republican. There's his picture when he was in the legislature. Oh that's right. We're not even pointing. We're trying to go with us. Listen I hear a thing.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: When was that taken? When was he in the legislature? When was he in the legislature?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do you know what his political beliefs were? You know what he wanted to see happen in the county and in the country?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Well no I don't know especially. He was I don't know. I wasn't paying much attention to it. But he was down there two years. Mother went with him one year. I don't know what he was working for. But whenever it was it was good a bit.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did he run on it? Do you know? Were you around when he was down?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Uh huh. You were still around.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What was his campaign like? What was the campaign like in those days?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: It was me. I don't know.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Oh you don't know?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I don't know.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do you think you don't remember whether you had to go out and shake hands or anything like that?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I don't know. I don't think he campaigned too much. Everyone knew him like it. Oh yeah. He was not all through this country. I don't think he campaigned too much. He might have a little. I can't remember. I wasn't very interested in politics.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I don't know.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do you remember what people's reaction to World War I was?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I remember a lot about it. What went on during World War I.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do you mean around here?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Well what we had to do and the prices of things and things like that.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What did you have to do?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Well you couldn't buy flour you know. Quite flour.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Why was that?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I don't know. I suppose they were shipping it over probably for the boys. If you bought a little sack of flour you'd have to buy oat flour and rice flour.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did they have whole wheat flour? Did they have whole wheat flour or not even in?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: It was hard stuff. I know that. Mother tried to bake with it and she couldn't make anything that was decent. And it was just awful. I don't know why. I never did know why that they were so short on white flour. Everything was so high too. Sugar and everything was so high.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were there any other things that you were ranching for?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: No. I guess you had the money you could buy. I don't know. I do remember bought flour because I always loved bread so well. My mother made such good bread and she couldn't make bread at all. She said I was the only one in the family. She felt sorry for me because I couldn't eat bread.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did she use a sourdough?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: She didn't use to make sourdough.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did she use yeast at all? With a sourdough?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I think you started with yeast. And then you just keep it from time to time and then you don't use it anymore.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: She's making the most delicious biscuits outside of my attitude. That's quite a thing now. A lot of the young people are going into the sourdough. I have a friend up here that makes sourdough biscuits.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: It's the mural.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: You would have been the one right before Summerfield. I don't know.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Anyway, they used to say that was Joe Collins. This guy wanted to kill. He killed a doctor. Then he went out here someplace and was in that house.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: He and his mother, wasn't it? Did he go to his mother's home?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Yeah.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I think I knew some of his relation to that fellow that did the killing.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Was his name Stephens?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: There's Kenneth Stephens here in Tonah. He had some relation to him. And then there was a George Stephens on his heart that was related to that god.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you ever hear then of why he killed Dr. Watkins

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Well, wasn't he looking for the sheriff to kill the sheriff?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I don't know. I've never read the book. All I've known is just what I hear people say. And you can get some funny ideas sometimes. So you heard that he was looking for the sheriff. That's what I heard. That he wanted to kill the sheriff. No, I don't know if that was true or not.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Is there any reason that anyone would have wanted to kill the sheriff?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I don't know. What his reason was.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I don't know whether the sheriff was trying to arrest him was here.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What did he do?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I think he did it out of the blue pretty much. Oh, I know.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: But I mean, why did he think the sheriff was after him?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I wonder. Had he done before that?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You think the sheriff was after him?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Well, that's what I understood. He thought the sheriff was after him.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Why did he kill the doctor?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: I'm asking you. You're not supposed to ask me.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I've never read the book, so I don't know.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Most people think that she did a pretty accurate job in that.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I don't know. I don't know a thing about it. It's just what I've heard.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What was this guy Joe Collins like? Who was the sheriff?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Oh, he was a great big man. He was the sheriff. Well, well, I think. Of course, I didn't know him when he was sheriff, but I used to talk to him. When I lived down on Foeman Road, he used to come up by there, and he was always out in the yard and always stopped talking to me. He was an elderly man. And he was sheriff. He was going, well, well, I understood someone to say that it was Collins that this fell wanted to kill. Now, that might be wrong, too. I never heard him say so.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did he ever tell you any stories about?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: He could have on that.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Yeah, I'm going to have to run.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You got along fine before that, huh?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Well, yes. And after the ball game was over, we were all good friends. We'd go to dances together and have the best time, and then next Sunday we'd be fighting again. Yeah, that was quite a rivalry there from Princeton Harbor. And all the young people in both towns were just real good friends, but there's something about those ball games that you have.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: When did the teams get going? They weren't around when you were really young?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Well, no. They started when I...

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: I must have been around 18, 16 or 18, when they started playing baseball.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did they actually have fights?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Oh, just... I don't think they were fought, really fought, but they really didn't talk bad to each other. I don't think I ever saw a fight. But you get awfully excited, you know, about your team winning.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: The same way with all the other towns.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were the teams pretty well matched?

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Yeah.

speaker: Ruby Wheeler
words: Sometimes we'd have... Princeton had a good time, so it's our team, and maybe we'd have a good team. And then Bogle and Dearie and all those towns, they got mixed up in it too.

speaker: Laura Schrager
